CONTENTS OF HEALTH EDUCATION
A> HUMAN BIOLOGY
Teaching of human biology starts from the kindergarten itself
The children are taught about the parts of the human body and
their functions.
They are also taught the importance of good health and methods
to keep physically fit.
Teaching is also directed towards the need far exercise, adequate
rest and sleep.
Information is also provided about the adverse effects Like
smoking use of alchohol, na etc.
B> NUTRITION
The main aim of including nutrition as a content of health
education is to help people choose optimum and balanced diets
which contain all the necessary nutrients for optimal health.
c> HYGIENE
The people are taught about the importance of hygiene and
methods of maintaining hygiene.
Hygiene is of 2 types
+
i> Personal Hygiene The aim is to promote good
standards of personal clearliness.
Measures of basic hygiene are taught here Like bathing
toothbrushing grooming etc.
ii> Environmental Hygiene - It comprises of 2 aspects
- domestic and community.
Domestic hygiene includes keeping the house and its
surroundings clean proper ventilation, proper
dispasal of waste, avoidance of pests.

community hygiene includes the care of the surroundings
ensuring proper garbage dispasal adequate sewage and
drainage.
D> FAMILY HEALTH CARE
People have to be taught about the importance of family health
care
The main aim here is to improve the health of the family as a unit
rather than an individual.
The importance of improving maternal oral health to improve
oral health of the child should also be stressed.
E> CONTROL OF COMMUNICABLE AND NON-COMMUNICABLE DISEASES
People have to be taught about the communicable and non-comm-
unicable diseases:
The aim is to provide elementary knowledge so that they can better
understand common signs and symptoms of disease and preve-
-ntion, thereby promoting health
F> PREVENTION OF ACCIDENTS
The people have to be taught about basic safety rules and how to
prevent common accidents, which take place in their homes, in
their work place or on the road.
G> USE OF HEALTH SERVICES
Provision of any form of health service will be a failure if the
people are not aware of the presence of such services.
Therefore, people have to be Informed about the various health
services and preventive programs available to them.
They also have to be educated on the proper use of these services.

BARRIERS IN COMMUNICATION
broo
A> PSYCHOLOGICAL BARRIERS
Emotional disturbances, depression, neurasis or any other
psychasomatic disorder can cause-psychológical barriers in
communication.
When interacting with individuals with psychological problems
special methods and utmost care should be adopted in order
to convey the message to them.
B> PHYSIOLOGICAL BARRIERS
They include difficulties in self-expression, difficulties in
hearing or seeing, difficulties in understanding etc.
While dealing with special groups Like deaf dumb or blind,
the channels of communication should be selected in such a
way as to target the group effectively DBONG
c> ENVIRONMENTAL BARRIERS
They are due to excessive noise difficulties in vision and conges-
-tion.
These can be overcome by making small groups & using proper
channels of comminication.
1> CULTURAL BARRIERS
Communication must take into account the cultural factors
like patterns of behaviour habits beliefs , customs etc
Care should be taken to send the message across effectively
without hurting the sentiments of the people.

The verbal barriers are -
attacking
its
interrogating
criticising
blaming
ordering
threatening
shouting
alk
The non-verbal barriers are
flashing eyes
rolling eyes
gestures out of exasperation
Keys to successful communication =
@TH personal contact is important EDBONG
always be courteous
be consistent and clear
Listen to others and show interest

1> @THE REARDEDBONG
- When an individual comes to the dental clinic or wellness centre
because of illness, the opportunity should be used to educate him
on matters of interest such as the cause and nature of illness,
its prevention, beneficial diet, oral hygiene etc.
Advantages
can be done in a dentists consultation room (2 way communi)
- cation
discussion, argument, persuation of an individual to change
his behaviour is possible
opportunity for the individual for asking questions and
clarifying doubts
Disadvantages
Benefits only small number of people

2> GROUP APPROACH
- Group education is an effective way of educating the community.
a> CHALK AND TALK (LECTURES)
- A lecture is defined as a carefully prepared oral presentation of facts,
organised thoughts and ideas by a qualified person.
- The lecture should have an opening statement which gives the theme
of the lecture
- Group should not be more than 30 people
- Duration of talk should not exceed 15-20 mins
- should be based on topic of current interest
- Disadvantage - one way communication. Learning is passive.
b> SYMPOSIUM
- A series of speeches on a selective topic
- Each speaker presents a brief aspect of the topic
a There is no discussion amongst speakers DBONG
- In the end, the audience may ask questions
- The chairman makes a summary at the end of the session
c> GROUP AISCUSSION
- A group is an aggregation of people interacting in a face to face situatis
- Process of identifying problems and finding solutions collectively by
members of group
- There should be a group leader who initiates the subject prevents
side conversations encourages everyone to participate and sums
up the discussion
- Disadvantage - there may be unequal participation. Some memb-
ers may be shy and some may be dominating.

3> MASS APPROACH
- Health education for a large community or the general public
can be given using the mass media of communication.
Advantages
- Large no. of people can be reached
- People of all socio economic status irrespective of their caste creed
and religion have access to health education
Disadvantages
- One way communication. Audience cant communicate with the
health educator.
a> TELEVISION
- Coverage to large number of people
- can also reach illiterate people
- can influence public opinion effectively
a - provides entertainment & education EDBONG
- Disadvantage is the high cast
b> RADIO
- Has a broader audience than television
- can also reach illiterate people
- Economical & easily accessible
c> NEWSPAPERS
Widely disseminated form of literature
N&
Disadvantage is the Low readership in rural areas because
of illiteracy
d> POSTERS
- They should catch the eye and must be artistic
- Message should be simple and short

- should be placed in cations where people frequent like bus stands,
hospitals
- they should be changed frequently to maintain their effect
e> INTERNET
- Very useful and accessible medium for mass spread of health
education to all age groups in the community
- The major disadvantage is chances of providing misleading
and unverified information.
- Thus, people should be advised to seek information only from
reputed websites & websites hasted by authorised organisations
@THE BEARDEDBONG

UNETHICAL PRACTICES IN DENTISTRY
1> Practice by unregistered persons employed by the dentist
2> Dentist signed under his name and authority issuing any
certificate which is untrue, improper and misleading
3> Dentist advertising whether directly or indirectly far the
purpose of obtaining patients or promoting his own professional
advantage
4> use of bogus diplomas etc
5> allowing commission
6> paying or accepting commission
7> undercutting of charges in order to solicit patients
8> if the planned treatment is beyond de the dentists skill, the
patient is not referred to a consultant
9> In case of an emergency consultation during the temporary
absence of patient's dentist Temporary service is provided &
G
the patient is not sent back to the original dentist.
10> If consulted, the dentist accepts charge of the case without request
of the referring dentist.

ETHICS
The word ethics is derived from Greek word ethas meaning
custom or character
Ethics is the philosophy of human conduct, a way of stating T019 and
evaluating principles by which problems of behaviour 507 can be
solved. Ethics is concerned with standards judging whether
actions are right or wrong.
PRINCIPLES OF ETHICS
SCLON
1> To do no harm (non-maleficence)
2> To do good (beneficence)
3> Respect far persons
4> Justice
5> Veracity or truthfulness
5> Confidentiality ARDEDBONG
1> TO DO NO HARM
To do no harm or non maleficence is considered to be the foundation
of social morality.
Although dental professionals support this principle in theory they
are at times guilty of transgressions that go beyond a limitation
Iatrogenic diseases is the name given to doctor-induced illness.
The dentist in cases where pain can't be avoided can make attempts
to minimise the pain.
2> TO DO GOOD
To do good or beneficence is required of all health care providers,
and it should be the role of dentists to benefit patients as well as
not to inflict harm.

In the process of treating the patient what has to be weighed are the
consequences of treatment versus no treatment.
processive worrd DIVISION
Attempts should be to maximise benefits and minimiseharm
3> RESPECT ONCE FOR PERSONS
std NOOD
It includes 2 fundamental considerations
i> AUTONOMY
Autonomy is a principle that dictates that health care professionals
respect the patient's right to make decisions concerning the
treatment plan.
Patients should not be bystanders in their treatment but active
participants.
Dentists sometimes attempt to direct a patient towards a
particular mode of treatment by stressing certain advantages
& not mentioning the disadvantages DBONG
It is a breach of ethics (unethical) to mislead or misinform
patients. In addition, it may well become a legal problema
ii> INFORMED CONSENT
Informed consent an essential component of a patients right
to autonomy is a two-step process
First information is presented to the patient by the doctor.
Secondly the patient satisfies himself / herself that he or
she understands, and based upon this understanding either
agrees or refuses to undergo the treatment.
Informed consent is the first stated and largest principle
of the Nuremberg code.
gross insurings

4) JUSTICE
The Isvipo primary duty of health professional is service Irrespective of class,
creed etc.
IN98N0
Justice demands that each person be treated equally.
The principle of justice calls for an obligation to protect the weak and
to ensure equity in rights and benefits.
Dentists can provide some free or discounted care in their offices
to those who are truely needy, or they can donate some time to
clinics for Low-income patients.
5>. TRUTHFULNESS
doni
The patient-doctor relationship is based on trust. Lying shows
disrespect to the patient and threatens relationship.
Veracity or truthfulness is an ethical principle that should be
unquestioned, yet many health professionals opt a to not follow
them in practice.
The dentist may feel that it would be better if the patient took
a certain course of action & therefore manipulates the inform-
ation that is given to the patient.
6> CONFIDENTIALITY
Patients have the right to expect that all communications &
records pertaining to their care will be treated as confidential
Information rea regarding the patient should not be disclosed
without the consent of the patient or an order from the
presiding judge in a Court of Law.

CONSENT
The term consent is defined as "when 2 or more persons agree
upon the same thing in the same sense they are said to consent
(as per the definition of consent given in section 13 of the Indian
Contract Act, 1872)
TYPES OF CONSENT
right
A. IMPLIED CONSENT
This is the most common variety of consent.
The fact that patient comes to a doctor for an ailment implies that
he is agreeable to medical examination in the general sense
This, however, does not imply consent to procedures more complex
than inspection, palpation, percussion, auscultation and routine
@THE sonography. BEARDEDBONG
B. EXPRESS CONSENT
Anything other than implied consent is express consent. This may
either be oral or written.
Express oral consent is obtained for relatively minor examinations
like or therapeutic procedures.
Express written consent is to be obtained for all major diagnostic
procedures, general anaesthesia and for surgical operations.
C. INFORMED CONSENT
SUN
Informed consent implies that all information must be
explained in comprehensible non-medical terms preferably
in Local Language about the -

ii> nature of treatment
iii> risks involved
iv> prospects of success
v> prognases if the procedure is not performed
vi> alternative methods of treatment
D. PROXY CONSENT
ALL the above types of consent can take siro the NOING shape of proxy will content.
Parent for child, clase relative for mentally unsound / unconscious
patient etc

COPRA
The Consumer Protection Act, 1986 that came into force on 15 th
April 1987 is one of the most progressive and comprehensive piece
of legislations enacted for the protection of consumers.
The main objective of the act is to provide for the better protection
of consumers.
Unlike other Laws which are punitive or preventive in nature,
the provisions of the act are compensatory in nature
The act is intended to provide simple, speedy & inexpensive re-
dressal to the consumers' grievances, and relief of a specific
nature & award of compensation wherever appropriate to the
consumer.
WHO IS LIABLE
All medical / dental practitioners doing independent medical
dental practice unless rendering only free service
Private hospitals charging all patients
ALL hospitals having free as well as paying patients and free
category patients receiving treatment in such hospitals.
Medical/dental practioners and hospitals paid by an insur-
ance firm far the treatment of a client
WHO IS NOT LIABLE
It exempts only those hospitals and the medical/dental
practitioners who offer free service to all patients.

STRUCTURE OF CONSUMER FORUM
The act propases a three tier quasi-judicial machinery at National,
State and District levels.
As per the Consumer Protection Rules 1987, a complaint filed in the
Consumer LONOILON Forum shall be adjudicated within a period of go days from
the bisco date of notice by oppasing party and within 150 days if it requires
analysis and testing of commodities
The claim amount at different Levels
National Commission - over 20 Lakhs
State Commission - over 5 Lakhs upto 20 Lakhs
District Forum
- upto Rs 5 Lakhs
The consumer protection (Amendment) Act, 1993 has introduced a
new section stating that the forums shall not admit a complaint
unless if it is filed within 2 years from the date of cause of action
Further, a complainant can present his case on his own without
the help of a lawyer.
The Consumer Protection (Amendment) Act, 2002 has increased
the claim amount at different levels.

NATIONAL ORAL HEALTH POLICY
The National Oral Health Policy has been formulated by DCI, through
the inputs of 2 national workshops organised in 1991 and 1994 at
Delhi and Mysare respectively.
These workshops considered the recommendations of national
workshops on oral health goals for India, Bombay 1984 and a
draft oral health policy prepared by IDA in 1986.
The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, GOI accepted in
principle the National Oral Health Policy in 1995 to be included
in the National Health Policy.
10 POINT RESOLUTION
1> There is an urgent need far an Oral Health Policy far the
nation as an integral part of the National Health Policy
NG
2> Special well coordinated National Oral Health Program be
Launched to provide oral health care, both in the rural as
well as in the urban areas due to deteriorating oral health
conditions in the country as revealed by various epidemio-
logical studies.
Dentist /population ratio in the rural areas is 1: 3,00,000,
whereas 80% children and 60% adults suffer from caries.
3> A past of full time dental Advisor at appropriate level in
the Directorate General of Health Services (Dte. G.H.S)
should be created as a first step towards strengthening
the Technical wing of Dte. G.H.S

4> Studies have revealed that dental diseases have been increasing both
in prevalence and severity over the Last few decades.
There is therefore an urgent need to prevent the rising trend of
dental diseases in India.
5> The Council, therefore resolves that preventive and promotive
oral health services be introduced from the village Level onwards
& a pilot project on oral health care be launched by MOHFW
during 1995-96 in 5 districts; 1 each in 5 states.
6> The Council further resolves that legislative measures be adapted
to ensure a statuatory warning on the wrappers & advertisement
of sweets, chocolates & other retentive sugar 0 eatables
TOO MUCH EATING SWEETS MAY LEAD TO DECAY OF TOOTH
7) The Council recommends that a National Training Centre be
established or the existing centres be strengthened far training
various categories of oral health care personnel.-
8> The Council also resolves that all District Hospitals & comm-
unity health centres have dental clinics
ALL dental colleges should have courses on Dental Hygienists
and Dental Technicians.
9> The Council further resolves. that the pilot project may be
extended to all states at the rate of one district in every
state.
10> The Council also resolves that there is an urgent to have a
National Institute for dental Research to guide oral health
research appropriate to the needs of the country

WHO
INTRODUCTION
The World Health Organisation is a specialised agency
within the charter of the United Nations which was establi-
shed for the purpose of co-operation amongst the member
countries & with others to promote the health of all people.
WHO serves as the directing and coordinating authority
for international health matters and public health.
ORIGINS
The Charter of the United Nations was adopted and signed
at the San Francisco conference in May 1946 1945.
Based on the draft proposed by Dr. Rene Sand & Dr.
In
Chris Brock Chisholm the World Health Conference met in
New York in June/July 1946 and adopted the Constitution webcove
of future World Health Organisation.
Thus WHO was officially barn on 7th April 1948 and 7th
April is celebrated as "World Health Day"
STRUCTURAL ORGANISATION
1> THE WORLD HEALTH ASSEMBLY
supreme decision making body far WHO
alota
main function is to determine the policies of the organisation
80
The Health Assembly appoints the Director - General,
supervises the financial policies of the Organisation &
reviews and approves the proposed program budget.

2> THE EXECUTIVE BOARD
The Executive Board is compased of 34 members technically qualified
in the field of health.
Main functions are to give effect to the decisions and policies of the
Health Assembly, to advise it and generally to facilitate its work.
3> THE SECRETARIAT
Headed by the Director-General, who is appointed for a 5 yearterm
by the Health Assembly on the nomination of the Executive Board.
Regional directors are appointed by the board with the agreement
of the relevant regional committee.
AGENDA
1> Promoting development
2> Fastering health security
3) Strengthening health systems DEDBONG
4> Harnessing rea research, information and evidence
5> Enhancing partnerships
6> Improving performance
WORK OF WHO
1> It acts as the directing and coordinating authority onall
international health work
2> Prevention and control of specific diseases
3> Development of comprehensive health services
4> Family Health
5> Environmental Health
6> Health statistics
7> Biomedical Research

8> Health literature & information
9> Cooperation with other organisations

@THE BEARDEDBONG

CHANGING CONCEPTS OF PUBLIC HEALTH
A> DISEASE CONTROL PHASE
- 1880 - 1920
- This phase aimed at the control of man's physical environment
eg - water supply sewage control
- These measures were not aimed at the control of any specific disease
- However, these measures vastly improved the health of the people
due to disease and death control.
B> HEALTH PROMOTIONAL PHASE
- 1920 1960
- This phase aimed at health promotion of individuals in addition to
disease control activities.
- It was initiated as personal health services such as mother and
child health services, school health services industrial health
services mental health and rehabilitation services.
- 2 important movements that were initiated during the early 20th
century were- -
a> Provision of basic health services through primary health
centres and sub centres for the rural and urban areas.
The concept of health centre was first quoted in 1920,
by Lard Dawson of England.
In 1931, the League of Nations Health Organisation called
for the establishment of health centres
b> The second greatme movement was the community develop-
ment program to promote village development through the
active participation of the whole community.

Although this program failed to survive establishment of
Primary Health Centres and Sub Centres provided the 0881 much
needed infrastructure for health services, specially in rural
areas.
c> SOCIAL ENGINEERING PHASE
- 1960 - 1980
- with advances in preventive medicine and practice of public health
the pattern of disease began to change in the developed world
- Many of the acute illness problems were solved, new health
problems in the form of chronic disease began to emerge,
eg - cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, alchoholism.
- These problems could not be tackled by the traditional approa-
ches to public health such as isolation, immunization and
G
disinfection nor could these be explained on the basis of the
Germ theory of disease.
- This Led public health into a new phase of called as social
engineering phase where social and behavioral aspects of
disease and health were given a new priority.
D> HEALTH FOR ALL PHASE
- 1981-2000
WHO in 1977 resolved that the main social target of
goverments & WHO should be the attainment of a Level of
health by all citizens of the world by 2000 that will
permit them to lead a socially & economically productive
Life.

- This culminated in the international objective of Health Far ALL by
the year 2000.
- On 12th Sept. 1978, the joint WHO- UNICEF International Confe-
rence at Alma-Ata called far acceptance of the WHO goal of
Health for ALL by 2000 and proclaimed Primary Health Care as
a way to achieving Health far ALL.
-
with the adoption of the goal of Health far ALL, a new public health
became evident world-wide, which may be defined as "the
organised application of Local, state, national and international
resources to achieve health for all

A> TOOTH BRUSHES
The Tooth brush is designed primarily to promote cleanliness of teeth
and oral cavity. (ADA Council on Dental Therapeutics)
Toothbrushes in conjunction with dentrifices are the most NG widely
used oral hygiene aids.
OBJECTIVES OF TOOTH BRUSHING
To clean teeth & interdental spaces of food remnants,
debris & stains etc
To prevent plaque farmation
To stimulate & massage gingival tissue
To clean the tongue
ADA SPECIFICATIONS OF TOOTH BRUSH
Brushing surface
1-1.25 inches in length
5/16 - 3/8 inches in width
it
2-4 rows of bristles
5-12 tufts/row

Interdental cleaning aids are adjunctive devices, which are used to

FACTORS IN SELECTION OF INTERDENTAL AID

> DENTAL services FLOSS
Dental Flass is indicated to remove plaque from interproximal

FUNCTIONS OF DENTAL FLOSS
Removal of adherent plaque & food debris from the interproximal
embrasure & under the pontics of the fixed partial denture
Polishing of the tooth surface during removal of plaque & debris
Stimulating & massaging the interdental papillar
Helping in locating - a> subgingival calculus
b> overhanging margins of restorations
c> proximal carious lesions
DISADVANTAGES
Time consuming
Requires skill
Carries the risk of tissue damage if not used properly
TECHNIQUES
Spool method
Circle or Loop method ARDEDBONG
2> INTERDENTAL BRUSH
They are cone shaped or cylindrical brushes made of bristles
mounted on a handle.
Particularly suitable for cleaning large, irrigular or concave
Tooth surfaces adjacent to wide interdental spaces.
3> WOODEN TIPS
Manufactured from orange wood and are triangular in crass
section, they are inserted into gingival embrasures with the
base of the triangle oriented towards the gingiva.
The wooden tip is then moved in and out of the embrasure,
thereby removing soft deposits from the teeth and also
mechanically stimulating the gingiva.

GINGIVAL MASSAGE
The mechanical stimulation of gingiva either by toothbrushing or
interdental cleansing leads to,
a> increased keratinisation
b> increased blood flow
c> increased flow of GCF within the gingival sulcus
WATER IRRIGATION DEVICES
Mainly beneficial in the removal of unattached plaque & debris,
irrigation devices are mainly compased of a built in pump &
resoi reservoir.
They may also be used to deliver antimicrobial agents eg. chlorhexidine
subgingivally.
@THE BEARDEDBONG

SCHOOL HEALTH SERVICES
School health services are defined as procedures established
a> to appraise the health status of pupils and school personnel
b> to council pupils, parents & others concerning appraisal
findings
c> to encourage the correction of remediable depects
d> to assist in the identification and education of handicapped
children
e> to help prevent & control disease
8> to provide emergency service for injury & sudden sickness
IDEAL REQUIREMENTS
A school oral health program should
a> be scientifically sound
be available to all children
c> b> provide the facts about dentistry & dental care, especially
about self care preventive procedures
d> aid in the development of favourable attitude towards
dental health
e> provide the environment necessary for the development
of psychomotor skills necessary for tooth brushing & flassing
f> include primary preventive dentistry programs - prophylaxis,
fluoride programs, & use of pit & fissure sealants
ASPECTS OF SCHOOL HEALTH SERVICE
a> Health Appraisal
b> Health Counselling
c> Emergency care & first aid
d> School Health education
e> Maintenance of school health records
f> Curative services

ELEMENTS/COMPONENTS
a> improving school -community relations
b> conducting dental inspections
c> conducting dental health education
d> performing specific programs
- -tooth brushing programs
— classroom based fluoride programs
- school water fluoridation programs
- nutrition as a part of school preventive dentistry program
- sealant placement
- science fare
- referral far dental care
ADVANTAGES
a> These programs can bring comprehensive dental care including
preventive measures to schoolchildren where they are gathered
for non dental reasons in the largest possible numbers.
This is particularly advantageous in dentist - deprived areas.
b> Students can be accessed during their formative years, from
childhood to adolescence
c> School clinics are less threatening than private offices since the
children are in familiar surroundings.
d> The children's daily contact with the dental personnel in other
roles may have a Lasting effect in their attitude towards
dentistry in general.
e> If the children can be maintained in a state of good dental
health it will be relatively easy to maintain their dental
health in adult life.
f> The expenses involved and the time used in transportation to
private dental office can be saved if the child gets dental
care in the school itself.

THETA PROGRAM
'THETA' stands for Teenage Health Education Teaching Assistants
program.
Developed by the National Foundation for the prevention of
oral disease for the us department of Health and Welfare
Division of Dental Health
PHILOSOPHY
Dental personnel teach high school children to teach preven-
Live dentistry to elementary school children
GOALS
To give knowledge and skills to young children
Allows high school children to develop understanding of
new children
a
Introduces BEARDEDBONG

ASKOV DENTAL DEMONSTRATION
Askov is a small farming community with a population mostly of
Danish extraction.
It showed very high dental caries in the initial surveys made in
1943 and 1946.
During the period from 1949 to 1957, the section on Dental Health
of the Minnesota Department of Health supervised a demonstration
school dental health program in ASKOV, including caries prevention
and control, dental health education and dental care.
ALL recognised methods for preventing dental caries were used
in the demonstration with the exception of community water
fluoridation since until 1955 Askov had no community water
supply.
Dental care was rendered by a group of 5 dentists from nearby
communities employed by the Minnesota Department of
Health. These dentists also gave topical fluoride treatments.
Findings available through a 10 year period revealed
- 28% reduction in dental caries in deciduous teeth of
children aged 3-5 years
- 34% reduction in caries in permanent teeth of children
6-12 years old
- 14% reduction in permanent teeth of children
13-17 years old
- improvements in filled-tooth ratios.

Jo The program also had many intangible benefits such as vala good
health and dietary habits for the children to carry on to
adult life.
JPI
900MT
spsvr
The cast of the program was greater and the caries reductions
smaller when compared with water fluoridation.
However fluoridation is by no means a substitute for such
a program
THE BEARDEDBONG

INCREMENTAL CARE
Incremental care may be defined as periodic care so spaced that
increments of dental disease are treated at the earliest time consis-
tent with proper diagnosis and operating efficiency in sucha way
that there is no accumulation of dental needs beyond the minimum
In private practice, six months is the commonest, though not the
only interval between visits.
In public health program, 1 year intervals are usually implemented
This represents the ideal pattern for care where appreciable incidence
of new dental disease is to be expected each year.
Treatment programs can be "gotten off the ground" by taking the
youngest available group in the first year and carrying it forward
in subsequent years as far as funds permit, each year adding a
new class of children at the next earliest available age until an
entire child population is being served to as high an age as available
resources permit.
ADVANTAGES
Lesions of dental caries are treated before there has been a chance
for pulpal involvement
Periodontal disease is intercepted at or near the beginning
Topical and other preventive measures are maintained on a
daily basis
Bills for dental services are equalised and regularly spaced
The program avoids the high expenditure of Late dental care
It confines dental disease to small early increments, thus
reducing lass of teeth.

It inculcates a habit of periodic return to the dental office in
subsequent years.
DISADVANTAGES
Time consuming
Attention to deciduous teeth
Increased likelihood of interruption in children's dental health
programs
@THE BEARDEDBONG

COMPREHENSIVE CARE
Comprehensive dental care is the meeting of accumulated dental needs
at the time a population group is taken into the program (initial care)
and the detection and carrection of new increments of dental
disease on a semiannual or other periodic basis (maintenance
care)
Preventing measures aimed to minimise disease are a part of
comprehensive dental care.
Services are provided not only to eliminate pain and infection
but also to
- restore serviceable teeth to good functional form
- replace missing teeth
we - provide maintenance care for the control of early lesions
of dental disease
T
provide preventive measures, educational and otherwise,
so that the population may experience a Lower prevalence
of disease
Dental care from WOMB to TOMB, this is comprehensive
dental care in the true sense.

PIT & FISSURE SEALANTS
Material that is introduced into the pits and fissures of caries
susceptible teeth, thus forming micromechanically bonded
protective layer cutting access of caries producing bacteria
from their source of nutrients are called pit and fissure sealants.
CLASSIFICATION
Pit & Fissure Sealants

Hybrid Sealants

INDICATIONS
children with recently erupted teeth with high caries risk
pasterior teeth specially molars with deep, narrow and retentive
pits and fissures
patient with signs of incipient caries

young patient receiving systemic or topical fluarides & still
highly caries prone
CONTRAINDICATIONS
children with Low caries risk i.e remained caries free for more
than 4 years
teeth with easy cleansable grooves and fassa
uncooperative patient
patient receiving non fluoridated water supply
PROCEDURE FOR SEALANT APPLICATION
Ideally, pits & fissures should be applied with sealants as soon as
the teeth erupts in the oral cavity
The tooth should be isolated with rubber dam or cotton rolls &
cleaned with purnice slurry
Tooth surface is rinsed & dried & etched with 37% phaspharic acid
gel far 15-30 sec. Properly acid etched enamel surface has frasted,
chalky white appearance after being dried with compressed air.
Self cured or Light cured sealant is mixed & applied with the
applicator into the pits & fissures.
Light activation for 15 sec
Rubber dam is removed
Occlusion is evaluated using articulating papers.
Any interfering contact is removed & tooth surface is polished.

A numerical value describing the relative status of a population

IDEAL REQUISITES
1> Clarity, Simplicity and Objectivity
The examiner should be able to remember the

The index should be simple and easy to apply

@TH field examinations RDEDBONG
The criteria for the index should be objective
& beinge um' unambiguous, with mutually exclusive
categories
2> Validity
The index must measure what it is intended to
measure & it should correspond with the clin-
ical stages of the disease under study at each
point
3> Reliability
The index should measure consistently at diff-
erent times and under a variety of conditions

4> Quantifiability
The index should be amenable to statistical analysis
5> Sensitivity
The index should be able to detect reasonably small
shifts, in either direction in the group conditions
6> Acceptability
The use of the index should not be painful or
demeaning to the subject. It should be universally
acceptable.

GINGIVAL INDEX
Gingival index was developed by Silness and Loe in 1963 for the purpose
of assessing the severity and Location of gingivitis by examining the
qualitative changes of the gingival soft tissue
The index shows good validity reliability and ease of use.
Although this index has demonstrated sufficient sensitivity, to distinguish
between mild & severe gingivitis, it may not discriminate as well
between the middle ranges.
INSTRUMENTS
Mouth mirror periodontal probe
METHOD
The severity of gingivitis is scared on all teeth or on selected index teeth
The index teeth BE 16 - Maxillary right first molar NG

24 - Maxillary Left first premolar
36 - Mandibular Left first mplar
32 - Mandibular left lateral incisor
44 - Mandibular right first premolar
Surfaces examined col Buccal, Lingual, mesial and distal
The teeth and gingiva should be dried Lightly awarp with JCO2 a blast seare of air ID and
cotton rolls.
A probe is used to press on the gingiva to determine its degree of
firmness, & to run along the soft tissue wall adjacent to the
entrance of the gingival sulcus.

CPITN PROBE
CPITN stands for Community Periodontal Index of Treatment
Needs.
Also called as WHO Periodontal Examination Probe and was first
described by WHO (TRS 621-1978)
This probe was designed for 2 purposes, namely measurement
of pocket depth and detection of subgingival calculus
The CPITN probe is both thin in the handle & is of very Light
weight (5 gins)
This probe is particularly designed for gentle manipulation of
the sensitive soft tissues around the teeth
STRUCTURAL DETAILS
The pocket depth is measured through color coding with a
black mark starting at 3.5 mm & ending at 5.5 mm.
a
The probe has a ball tip of 0.5 mm diameter that allows
NG
easy detection of subgingival calculus. This feature combined
with the Light probe weight facilitates the identification of the
base of the pocket thus decreasing the tendency for false read-
ing by over measurement.
A variant of this basic probe has 2 additional lines at 8.5 mm
& 11.5 mm from the working tip. They are useful far performing
a detailed assessment & recording of deep pockets for making
treatment plans for complex periodontal therapy.
The joint working committee of WHO/FDI have advised the
manufacturers of CPITN probes to identify the instruments
as CPITN-E for the epidemiological probe with 3.5 mm &
5.5 mm markings, or CPITN -C for the clinical probe with
additional 8.5 mm & 11.5 mm markings.

DENTAL AUXILLARY
DEFINITION
A dental auxiliary is a person who is given responsibility by a
dentist so that he or she can help the dentist render dental
care, but who is not himself or herself qualified with a dental
degree.
The duties undertaken by dental auxiliaries range from
simple tasks such as sarting instruments to relatively comp-
Lex procedures which form part of the treatment of patients.

FOUR HANDED DENTISTRY
The term four.handed dentistry is given to the art of seating
both the dentist and the dental assistant in such a way that
both SETTLEN are within easy reach of the patient's mouth.
The patient is in a fully supine position. The assistant will
hand the dentist, the particular instrument she needs
She will also perform additional tasks such as retraction
or aspiration.
The dentist can thus keep his hands & eyes in the field of ope-
ration and work with Less fatigue & greater efficiency
Dentists who do not expect too much from their assistants
prefer to train them on the job. Training courses do exist,
extending over a period of one year to 2 years.
@THE BEARDEDBONG
DUTIES OF DENTAL ASSISTANTS
Reception of the patient
Preparation of the patient for any treatment he
or she may need
Preparation & provision of all necessary facilities
such as mouthwashes & napkins
Sterilisation, care & preparation of instruments
Preparation & mixing of restorative materials
including both filling & impression materials
Care of the patient after treatment until he or she
leaves
Preparation of the surgery for the next patient
Assistance with x-ray work & processing and
mounting of x-rays

SCHOOL DENTAL NURSE
This is an operating auxiliary, who is permitted to diagnase dental
disease & to plan and carry out certain specified preventive and
treatment measure, including some operative procedures in the
treatment of dental caries and periodontal disease in defined groups
of people, usually school children.
T.A. Hunter, founder of the New Zealand Dental Association influen-
ced the formation of Dental Nurse Scheme
The Dental Nurse Scheme was established in Wellington, New
Zealand in 1921 due to the extensive dental diseases found in army
recruits during World War 1.
The training extends to over a period of 2 years to cover both the
reversible & Prreversible procedures.
Upon completion of course training, each school dental nurse is
assigned to a school where she is employed by the government
to provide regular dental care for 450-700 children
In New Zealand, the dental nurses are predominantly in the school-
based salaried service & are expected to provide care to the children
at nearby 6-month intervals.
DUTIES
a> Oral Examination
b> Topical Fluoride Application
c> Prophylaxis
d> Advice on dietary fluoride supplements
e> Administration of Local anaesthetic

DENTAL HYGIENIST
Dental hygienist is a person, not being a dentist or a medical practi-
tioner, who does oral prophylaxis, gives instructions in oral hygiene
& preventive dentistry assists the dental surgeon in chairside work
and manages the office. He/she shall work under the supervision
of the dental surgeon.
Dr. Alfred Civilian Fones is considered the father of dental
hygiene. He is also credited with the first training school for
dental hygienists, in Nov 1913.
The duration of the course was far 7 months.
The Dental Council of India has prescribed that
a> The course of study should extend over a period of 2 academic
years & lead to the qualification of dental hygienist certifi-
b> The candidate should be atleast 15 years of age at the time cate. of
admission or within 3 months of it & should be medically
fit.
c> The candidate must have passed matriculation from a
recognised university with science as a subject.
DUTIES
1. Cleaning of mouths & teeth with particular attention to calculus and
stains
2. Topical application of fluorides, sealants & other prophylactic solns.
3. Screening or preliminary examination of patients as individuals
or in groups, such as school children or industrial employees , so
that they may be referred to a dentist for treatment.
4. Instruction in oral hygiene

@THE BEARDEDBONG

SHOE LEATHER SURVEY
In 1931, U.S Public Health Service appointed Dr Trendly H Dean
to continue the work of Dr. Fredrick Mckay in determining the
extent and severity of mottled enamel.
Dean conducted a survey among 22 cities in ten states of USA on
a total population sample of 5824 children and gave the following
report on mottling of enamel at various concentrations of fluoride.
This survey which established Fluoride as the causative factor for
mottling of enamel was known as Shoe Leather Survey.
FINDINGS
1. A high concentration of fluoride in water is directly
related to the severity of enamel mottling
2. Enamel mottling was widespread in areas with water
having fluoride content at 3 ppT EDBONG
3. Mottling with discrete pitting of enamel was noticed
at fluoride levels of 4 ppm
4. Mottling was less in case of fluoride levels of 2.5- ppm
with a dull chalky white appearance of teeth
5. No mottling or any other enamel changes were obs-
erved in areas with water containing 1 ppm fluoride
Based on the findings and detailed statistical analysis, Dean
developed a standard system for classification of dental
fluarasis, called as "mottling index" ( Dean's Index of
Fluarasis ). (1934)

Another characteristic feature observed was that mottled enamel did
not develop dental caries compared to normal enamel, which was highly
susceptible to caries
Ni
In 1942, the important milestone discovery was Latrab made by Dean & his
colleagues that at 1 ppm F in drinking water, a 60% reduction in
caries experience was observed
In 1969, WHO advocated that 1 ppm of fluoride in community water
supplies was a practical and effective public health measure.
(suphotal

DEFLUORIDATION OF WATER
Defluaridation is the process of removing excess naturally occuring
fluoride from drinking water in order to reduce the prevalance &
severity Nose of dental fluarasis.

a> Based upon ion exchange process or adsarption each
b> Based upon addition of chemicals to water during treatment

drawback is regeneration & maintenance of the
plant.
NALGONDA TECHNIQUE
Developed by the National Environmental Engineering Research
Institute (NEERI) at Nagpur in 1974.
The process is extremely useful for domestic as well as for
community water supplies.

The process comprises addition in sequence of sodium aluminate
time and bleaching powder to the fluoride water followed by flocc-
ulation, sedimentation and filtration

speed of 10-20 rpm so that the coagulant is rapidly
& uniformly dispersed.
